segments.s071-g65: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. مهراب را places Mehrab (here a proper name) marked by the object/topic marker را — in older epic diction names can take را to emphasize the person. کهتری‌گر is a descriptive compound meaning 'more lowly / humbler' (a diminisher), and همال is a noun 'attendant, porter'; together they form a nominal phrase addressing the addressee as 'the humblest attendant of Mehrab'. Word order is emphatic: pronoun → named person + را → epithet.
segments.s071-g66: مر here is the dative/goal 'to me' (i.e. 'tell me'). آن دخت 'that maiden' is a determiner + noun phrase; او را is a resumptive/enfatic pronoun with را marking it (literally 'his/that one'), linking the girl to her father. کجا دید is the question 'where did (he) see' — دید is past-tense 'saw' — and زال is the subject at the end (typical Persian SOV order: object/phrase ... verb ... subject). So the clause asks Zal where he saw that maiden.
segments.s071-g67: This is a linked series introduced by repeated به ('with/in/regarding') before each noun for rhythmic and emphatic balance: به روی (about her face) و به موی (and her hair) و به خوی (and her temperament) و خرد (and her wisdom). The final noun خرد is coordinated into the same frame. The repetition of به is a poetic device marking the topics to be described.
segments.s071-g68: به من گوی is an imperative/request 'tell me' (به + me + verb). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that' (تا باکی اندر خورد = 'so that no worry/harm falls upon [us]'). باکی = 'worry/objection', اندر = 'in/within', and خورد here is the archaic perfective/passive sense 'befall/come to pass' (not 'eat'). The clause is therefore a purposive clause: tell me in order that no anxiety may befall.
segments.s071-g69: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the realms to be described: بالا (literally 'height' or 'stature/loftiness'), دیدار (appearance/meeting or the sight of him), و فرهنگ (his culture/upbringing). اوی is an archaic third‑person possessive/pronoun 'his'. The phrase lists qualities of the man whose attributes are to be recounted.
segments.s071-g70: بران سان 'in/according to that manner' (بران = 'thereon/upon that', سان = 'such/like so') introduces the manner clause. که دیدی 'which you saw' — که is the relative/connector and دیدی is the second‑person past 'you saw'. یکایک بگوی is an imperative formula 'speak/tell one by one' (یکایک = 'one by one, each in turn'; بگوی here functions as an exhortation to enumerate). Overall: 'Describe those things as you saw them, item by item.'
segments.s071-g71: بدو گفت is 'she/one said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', گفت is the past 'said'); the sequence reverses modern colloquial order but is standard in epic diction. سیندخت is the speaker's name; کای پهلوان is a vocative phrase: کای is the archaic vocative 'O' (or the honorific 'Kay') and پهلوان 'hero/champion'. So this segment introduces direct speech addressed to a champion.
segments.s071-g72: This is a string of honorific epithets in apposition to the addressee: سر پهلوانان 'head/chief of the champions' (سر here = 'chief, leader'), و پشت گوان 'and the support/bulwark of men' (پشت = 'support/back; protection', گوان = 'folk/men/thus' — here 'of men'). The nouns are placed after the vocative to amplify praise; word order is nominal and appositive.
segments.s071-g17: زمین را (with the object marker را) + ببوسید is a past-tense 3rd-person verb: 'he kissed the earth.' The conjunction و links this clause to the next: کرد آفرین is a light-verb construction where کرد (past 3rd) takes آفرین as its object/phrase, here meaning 'uttered/poured forth an exclamation of praise.' The word order (object/phrase then کرد) is a poetic inversion but grammatical.
segments.s071-g18: This continuation supplies the targets of the exclamation. آفرین is understood from the previous clause; ابر شاه و بر پهلوان زمین names two recipients of the praise. ابر شاه is a poetic epithet for the king, و = 'and', and بر introduces the second beneficiary (آفرین بر پهلوان زمین = 'praise to the hero of the land'). زمین here specifies 'of the land/earth.'
segments.s071-g19: A simple coordinated list of nouns joined by و (and): نثار (offerings), پرستنده (worshippers/devotees), اسپ (steeds), پیل (elephants). In poetic prose the plural is often implied; the list functions as the direct objects or items being displayed/placed in the next phrase.
segments.s071-g20: رده (a row/series) is the noun subject; بر کشیده is a participial/verbal phrase from برکشیدن meaning 'to draw up/extend' — کشیده is the past participle 'drawn/extended' and بر functions as the verbal prefix/preposition. ز در is the poetic az ('from the gate'), and تا دو میل gives the extent 'as far as two miles.' The whole phrase describes the arrangement/extent of the ranks.
segments.s071-g21: یکایک 'one by one' modifies همه 'all/everyone'; پیش سام is a prepositional phrase 'before Sam'; آورید is a 2nd-person plural/formal imperative of آ وردن 'bring' (command): 'Bring them all, one by one, before Sam.' Word order places the adverbial first, then the group, then the prepositional phrase, ending with the imperative verb.
segments.s071-g22: سر پهلوان ('the hero's head') is a poetic way to say 'the hero' as subject; خیره شد is past passive/experiential 'became astonished/stunned.' کان is an archaic relative/demonstrative ('that/what' — 'that which'), and بدید is past 3rd-sing of دیدن 'he saw.' Together: 'the hero was astonished at what he saw.'
segments.s071-g23: پر اندیشه بنشست is a compact descriptive clause: پر (here 'full (of)') modifies اندیشه ('thought'), and بنشست is the simple past 'sat down' — word order is modifier + noun + verb. برسان مست is a simile: برسان (here written merged; in classical usage 'بر سان') functions as 'like/as' and مست is the noun/adjective 'drunk' describing the manner of sitting.
segments.s071-g24: بکش کرده دست is a verbal sequence: بکش (here with the sense 'pull/draw') plus کرده forming a perfect-participial or resultative phrase meaning 'having drawn/pulled (his) hand'. و connects the second descriptive predicate; سرافگنده پست are two adjectives in apposition meaning 'ashamed' and 'low/humbled', describing his state after that action.
segments.s071-g33: برید is an imperative/command form (archaic/classical usage) meaning 'take' or 'bring' (second-person plural/polite). این is the demonstrative 'this'. به + گنجور دستان forms a prepositional phrase 'into the hands of the treasury/treasurer' (گنجور = treasury, دستان = hands, i.e. the person who holds the treasury). دهید is the imperative 'give' agreeing with برید, so the clause means 'Take this and give it into the treasurer's hands.'
segments.s071-g34: به نام functions as the idiom 'in the name of / under the name/account of'. مه کابلستان here is treated as a name or title ('Mah of Kabulistan' — a poetic epithet), specifying for whose account or under what heading the gift is to be given. دهید is again the imperative 'give', repeating/completing the instruction. Grammatically, this clause modifies or specifies the recipient/account for the gift.
segments.s071-g35: پری روی is a compound adjective meaning 'fair-faced' or 'like a fairy in visage' (adjective preceding the name). سیندخت is the proper name. بر پیش سام is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of Sam' (بر + پیش = 'in front of / before'). The clause sets the scene: 'Sindukht, fair of face, stood before Sam.'
segments.s071-g36: زبان کرد is a classical verbal compound where زبان ('speech/utterance') combines with the light verb کرد ('did/made') to mean 'she spoke' or 'she opened her tongue'. گویا here means 'eloquent' / 'expressive' modifying how she spoke. و دل شادکام links with 'and her heart was joyful' (شادکام = joyful/fortunate). The verbs are compressed poetically; the light verb construction makes the action 'to speak' explicit.
segments.s071-g37: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. آن هدیه‌ها را is the definite object 'those gifts' with the direct-object marker را. پذیرفته is a perfect/perfect-participle 'accepted/received' and دید ('saw') follows as the main verb — a common classical pattern where a participle + دید expresses 'saw that X had been done' (i.e., 'when she saw that those gifts had been accepted').
segments.s071-g38: رسیده ... دید mirrors the previous participle+دید pattern: رسیده 'having arrived' + دید 'she saw' = 'she saw that (something) had arrived'. بهی ('good' / 'well-being') and بدی ('evil' / 'misfortune') are juxtaposed: 'she saw that good had arrived and bad had departed.' رفته is the participle 'gone' modifying بدی. The sequence is concise and poetic but follows the participle + دید construction.
segments.s071-g39: سه بت روی 'three idol-faced' is an idiomatic poetic expression meaning 'three beauties' (بت = beauty/idol, روی = faced). با او 'with her' and به یک جا 'in one place/together' specify their accompaniment and location. بدند is an archaic past/plural form meaning 'they were' (classical form of بودن). So the clause states 'three beautiful-faced women were together with her.'
segments.s071-g40: سمن پیکر is a poetic compound 'jasmine-formed' (smen = jasmine, پیکر = figure/shape), describing delicate form; و سرو بالا 'and lofty as cypresses' (سرو = cypress, بالا = tall/lofty). بدند repeats the archaic 'they were'. The two nominal compounds are descriptive predicates characterizing the women's appearance.
segments.s071-g57: همه شهر زنده places the quantifier همه before شهر to mean 'all/everyone of the city'; زنده ('living') follows to qualify those people. برای + تواند here shows possibility/ability in relation to a beneficiary (implicit 'him'): تواند is the third-person present/subjunctive/potential of توانستن, meaning 'can/may be.'
segments.s071-g58: پرستنده and خاک پای are two nominal predicates coordinated by و; the final تواند governs both, so the structure reads 'can be (a) worshipper and (can be) the dust of the foot.' خاک پای is a possessive/genitive phrase 'dust of the foot' (humble metaphor).
segments.s071-g59: ازان ('from that') introduces a cause or source; ترس is the noun 'fear.' The particle کو functions as a relative/linking word (classical usage like 'که' = 'which/that') connecting ترس to the verb at the end; آفرید is perfect/past 'created' and takes هوش و زور ('intellect and might') as its objects.
segments.s071-g60: درخشنده is an adjective 'radiant/shining' placed before the coordinated proper names ناهید و هور; آفرید again is the past-tense verb 'created.' Adjective–noun order (modifier before noun) is common in classical Persian poetry for emphasis.
segments.s071-g61: نیاید is a negative jussive/optative meaning 'should not be' or 'it does not become'; it governs the nominal subject چنین کارش ('such a deed, his deed'). از تو پسند literally 'pleasing from you' = 'approved by you' (از marks the agent/experiencer with پسند 'approval').
segments.s071-g62: میان را is the definite object (را after میان = 'the middle/way'); به خون ریختن is a gerund/infinitival phrase 'by/with shedding blood' giving the means or manner (به ... -ly/by). در مبند is a negative imperative ('do not close/fasten/shut'): مبند = 'do not close' and در here refers to the passage/way (classical usage 'در' = door/gate/passage), so the clause warns 'do not block the passage by shedding blood.'
segments.s071-g63: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بد + او contracted) marking the indirect object; سام یل is a name plus epithet 'Sam, the hero.' گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing direct speech, and با من بگوی is an imperative/command 'speak/tell with me' (با + من = 'to/with me'; بگوی here is the imperative/hortative form of گفتن 'say/tell').
segments.s071-g64: ازان کت بپرسم: ازان ('about/from that') + کت ('coat') with بپرسم ('I will ask' or 'let me ask' — first-person subjunctive/optative of پرسیدن) expresses intent to inquire about the coat. بهانه مجوی is a negative imperative 'do not seek an excuse' (مجوی = 'do not seek'), placed as a separate admonition following the request.
segments.s071-g113: This clause lists the people included: تو ('you') is the nominative/topic. با + کابل (with Kabul) and و هر که (and whoever / whomever) introduce joint participants. پیوند here means 'bond' or 'tie' and syntactically attaches to the relative clause که, so پیوندِ (the bond) belongs to the person indicated; تست in this line is not treated as the English verb 'test' — none of the provided senses fit the classical possessive/archaic ending that appears here, so it likely functions as an archaic 2nd‑person enclitic/ending (see senses mapping).
segments.s071-g114: بمانید is the imperative/optative or jussive plural/2nd‑person plural form of ماندن 'to remain/continue' addressed to the previously listed addressees. شادان دل is a compound expression meaning 'joyful in heart' (شادان modifies state of دل). و connects to تن‌درست, a compound adjective 'healthy of body' (تن‌درست = 'sound/healthy'), so the predicate applies to the people named in the first clause.
segments.s071-g115: بدین here is the fused prepositional phrase 'به + این' ('with/in this matter'); نیز 'also/likewise' emphasizes agreement; همداستانم is a first‑person present statement 'I agree / I am of the same mind'. که introduces the content clause that follows. زال is the proper name and the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s071-g116: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. گیتی 'the world' is the source or realm. چو functions temporally as 'when'. رودابه is the proper name object. جوید is the present tense of جستن 'to seek' (3rd person singular), governing the object رودابه. همال in classical usage would mean 'a match / equal' (here 'a suitable partner'), but the provided senses do not list that meaning so it is marked accordingly.
segments.s071-g117: شما is the polite/plural 'you'. گرچه introduces a concessive clause 'although / though'. از + گوهر literally 'of/from essence' expresses difference of nature or origin (here 'of different substance'). دیگرید is a second‑person plural/polite present form meaning 'you are different' (literally 'are other').
segments.s071-g118: همان here functions as 'that very' / 'nevertheless' pointing to what follows. تاج و اورنگ 'crown and throne' form the definite object; را is the object marker attaching to the phrase. در خورید is a verbal phrase where خورید (from خوردن in a literary sense) means 'befall/come to you' or 'to receive/wear' — idiomatically 'you shall have/take' — and در is part of the fixed collocation 'درخور / در خوریدن' (to be fitting or to be given).
segments.s071-g119: چنین است is a formulaic 'thus is / such is'. گیتی is the topic 'the world' and follows with a comment. وزین here qualifies ننگ ('weighty/serious shame' or 'grave disgrace'); ننگ نیست states 'there is no shame' or 'no disgrace is (present/appropriate)' — the negative refers to the absence or nonexistence of a certain kind of shame in the world. Word order places the predicate after the topic in the elevated poetic style.
segments.s071-g120: ابا as used here is verbal/nounal but functions as 'refusal/reluctance'; with the negation structure that follows the line reads as 'the Creator of the world has no reluctance regarding war' or more idiomatically 'the Creator of the world does not shrink from battle.' کردگار جهان is a genitival phrase 'the Creator of the world' (the agent/topic), and جنگ نیست 'there is no (such) war‑aversion' — the negation applies to ابا.
segments.s071-g1: چو at the start is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شد + ساخته form a passive/perfect-like construction: شد is the auxiliary 'became/was' and ساخته is a past participle 'made/finished', so 'چو شد ساخته کار خود' = 'when his work had been finished'. کار خود places the affected thing ('his work') before the verb. برنشست is the compound verb: بر (on/upon) + نشست (sat) = 'sat down/settled'.
segments.s071-g2: چو again introduces a temporal clause 'when'. گردی here is a noun functioning as the thing wrapped/placed (a circular band / girdle in context), followed by the beneficiary/target marked by به مردی ('to/around a man'). میان را is the definite object 'the middle/waist' with the object marker را, and ببست is the past tense 'he bound/tied/closed'. Grammatically the clause says: 'When (he) girded/put a ring on the man's middle, he bound it.' Note: گردی is a concrete noun (a circular thing) in this context rather than the senses listed for 'dust' or 'wandering'.
segments.s071-g3: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one'/'someone'. ترگ رومی is a noun + modifier 'a Roman rag' (ترگ = rag, رومی = Roman/Byzantine). به سر + بر نهاد is a verbal phrase: بر (on/upon) + نهاد (past of 'place/put') = 'placed on the head'. Word order places the object ('a Roman rag') before the verb phrase in typical Persian poetic style.
segments.s071-g4: یکی again = 'another/one (of them)'. باره here means 'an occasion/at one stroke' (a single action/instance). زیراندرش is a fused locative 'زیر اندر + -ش' meaning 'under him/underneath it'. همچو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (باد = wind). The whole segment says that another (one) moved/acted beneath him with the swiftness or effect of the wind.
segments.s071-g5: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. گرازان is a collective plural noun 'boars' (a troop/pack of boars). به درگاه سام is a prepositional phrase 'to Sam's court/threshold' (درگاه used as 'royal court' or 'audience-hall'), with Sam as the proper name following it (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry).
segments.s071-g6: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. آواز داد is an idiom 'gave a voice' = 'uttered a cry'; داد here is the past 'gave'. برگفت نام uses برگفت (past of بر گفتن) meaning 'uttered/said' and نام = 'name'. Together: 'they neither gave a cry nor spoke a name.'
segments.s071-g7: به کار آگهان گفت is a dative + verb construction: به کار (to the business/officials) + آگهان (here functioning as the addressed group; the text's form is archaic/poetic) and گفت is the past 'he said/told'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that', and ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly' modifying the verb in that subordinate clause. Overall: 'He told (them) so that suddenly ...' Note: آگهان appears in a poetic form; the clause structure is 'X گفت تا Y' with تا + subjunctive expressing purpose.
segments.s071-g8: بگویند is the present-subjunctive/optative plural 'let them say / they should proclaim' (purpose dependent on the preceding تا). با here functions as a preposition 'to/at/for' in the sense of addressing someone, سرفراز is an epithet 'the proud/victorious one', and جهان is 'the world' — together 'to the world's proud one' or 'to the proud champion of the world'. Grammatically this is the content of the purpose clause introduced by تا: 'so that they should suddenly proclaim it to the world's proud one.'
segments.s071-g25: This clause is a series of interrogative/location words followed by a nominal predicate. 'که' here functions as a linking/introductory particle; 'جایی کجا' is a poetic doubling meaning 'in what place / where'; 'مایه' is the noun 'resource/provision' and 'چندین' qualifies it as 'many/several'; 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. Word order is standard old/poetic Persian: (particle) + locative phrase + predicate + copula.
segments.s071-g26: 'فرستادن' is the verbal noun/infinitive 'sending'; 'زن' is the object 'a woman'; 'چه' is an interrogative 'what/what kind of'; 'آیین' is 'custom, practice'; 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The phrase asks about the nature of the practice of sending a woman (infinitive + interrogative + predicate).
segments.s071-g27: 'گراین' is the conditional particle 'گر این' (= 'if this') introducing the protasis; 'خواسته' = 'this request/wish'; 'ز' (from) + 'و' (here standing for 'او' = 'him/her' though split in the word list) marks the source 'from him'; 'پذیرم' is first person singular present/subjunctive 'I accept' used in the protasis 'if I accept'; 'همه' qualifies the following consequence ('all' / 'entirely').
segments.s071-g28: This is the apodosis describing the consequence: 'ز من' = 'because of/from me' (prepositional phrase), 'گردد' is a present/subjunctive/impersonal form 'becomes' or 'will become', 'آزرده' = 'distressed/displeased', 'شاه رمه' = 'the king's host/multitude' (compound noun phrase where 'شاه' qualifies the following noun). Word order is poetic: prepositional phrase + verb + adjective + noun group.
segments.s071-g29: 'وگر' = 'and if' introduces a conditional clause. 'بازگردانم' is first person singular present/subjunctive 'I return / give back' (used here in the protasis). 'از پیش زال' is a prepositional phrase: 'از' = 'from', 'پیش' = 'in the presence of / before', and 'زال' is the proper name Zal; together they indicate returning someone into Zal's presence or back to Zal.
segments.s071-g30: 'برآرد' is third person singular present/future narrative 'he/it brings forth / raises'. 'به کردار' is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/like' (به + noun-of-manner); 'سیمرغ' is the mythical bird used as the model, and 'بال' 'wing' is the thing raised. The clause states the expected action in the apodosis: he will lift/raise wings in the manner of the Simurgh.
segments.s071-g31: 'برآورد' here is a narrative verb meaning 'he raised / he brought up'; 'سر' 'his head' follows the verb (poetic word order V + O) to mean 'he lifted his head'; 'گفت' 'said' introduces reported speech; 'کاین' = 'that this' (= 'that which is/this is'); 'خواسته' = 'the request/wish' (object of 'گفت'). The clause frames the speaker preparing to state the content of the request.
segments.s071-g32: This is the content of the reported request: a simple noun phrase 'غلامان و پیلان' = 'servants and elephants' linked by 'و' ('and'), followed by the adjective/past-participle 'آراسته' = 'adorned/decorated' describing them. In classical Persian the adjective follows the noun as here.
segments.s071-g81: This is a noun phrase: نهفته is an adjective 'hidden' qualifying the following noun phrase. همه گنج means 'the whole/all the treasure(s)'; کابلستان is the place-name modifying گنج (classical/poetic Persian often omits the linking -e/ezāfe). Word order (adjective + quantifier + noun + place) is idiomatic in Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s071-g82: بکوشم is the 1st-person singular present/subjunctive with the prefix be- used here to express volition/intent ('I will try/I will strive'). It is followed by another verb in the same form, رسانم, which functions like a purpose clause ('to bring/deliver'); the direct object (the treasures) is omitted because it was just stated. به زابلستان is a prepositional phrase 'to Zabulistan' indicating destination.
segments.s071-g83: جزین نیز is a concessive/contrastive opening: جزین 'apart from/except this' with نیز 'also/too' for emphasis. هر چیز کاندر خورد is a relative clause: هر چیز 'any/each thing' + کاندر ('that in which' / 'which in') + خورد used in the sense 'befalls/occurs', so the whole means 'anything that may happen/occur.'
segments.s071-g84: ببیند is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of دیدن used jussively ('let him see/let him learn' or 'may he know'). ز من means 'from me' (ز = from, poetic). مهتر پر خرد is an appositive nominal phrase: مهتر 'the chief/elder' qualified by پر خرد 'full of wisdom' (پر = 'full (of)', خرد = 'wisdom'). Together: 'let the wise chief learn/know it from me.'
segments.s071-g85: گرفت is a simple past 3rd-person singular 'took/ seized'. آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' (آن here = 'then'). The subject سام follows the time phrase (poetic word order). دستش 'his hand' uses the enclitic ش for possession; به دست 'into the hand' repeats دست as the instrument/position: together 'took his hand in his hand' (i.e. grasped his hand).
segments.s071-g86: ورا is a dative 'to him/unto him'. نیک is an adverb/adjective here meaning 'well/kindly'. بنواخت (from نواختن) is used idiomatically: literally it can mean 'struck/played' but in this context it conveys a hospitable, kindly reception (he greeted/entertained him). و links the clauses; پیمان ببست is a verbal phrase 'he bound/tied a pledge' (ببست = bound), i.e. concluded a covenant.
segments.s071-g87: چو + past verb بشنید = 'when she heard'. سیندخت is the subject (proper name). سوگند او is the object 'his oath' (او = his). The clause is a temporal subordinate clause ('When Sindukht heard his oath ...').
segments.s071-g88: همان acts as a copular/emphatic demonstrative 'that very / the same' linking to what was heard. راست‌گفتار 'truthful speech' and پیوند 'bond/pledge' describe the oath; او again marks possession 'his'. In context this affirms that his oath was indeed true and binding.
segments.s071-g105: This is an address to Kabul using the negative imperative مسوز (from سوزدن) meaning “do not burn/afflict.” دل بیگناهان is a genitival phrase “the hearts of the innocent” (دل + بیگناهان), placed before the verb in typical Persian order; the verb مسوز negates harming them.
segments.s071-g106: کجا … آید is an interrogative clause: کجا ‘where/how’ + آید (present/subjunctive-like sense ‘come, enter’). تیره‌روز اندر آید به‌روز is poetic: تیره‌روز ‘the ill‑fated’ + اندر آید ‘may enter into’ + به‌روز ‘the day/ daylight/good day’ — i.e. ‘how could the ill‑fated come into a bright day?’
segments.s071-g107: چو بشنید is a temporal clause: چو ‘when’ + بشنید (past tense ‘he heard’). سخنها is the object/subject ‘the words’ and ازو ‘from him’ marks the source; پهلوان placed at the end identifies who heard (the hero). Word order is typical poetic inversion: object/time + verb + agent.
segments.s071-g108: دید is the past tense verb ‘he saw/beheld.’ زنی is the direct object ‘a woman.’ با رای و روشن‌روان is a descriptive phrase ‘with (or of) counsel and bright‑soul’ (با + two nouns linked by و) giving her qualities; با here means ‘with/possessing.’
segments.s071-g109: This is a string of similes: به رخ چون بهار ‘in countenance like spring’ and به بالا چو سرو ‘in height like a cypress.’ به before رخ and بالا marks the domain (‘in/with respect to’) and the two چو‑clauses are standard comparative similes.
segments.s071-g110: میانش چو غرو gives another simile ‘her middle (میانش) like the sunset (غرو).’ The conjunction و links the next clause به رفتن تذرو, where به رفتن uses رفتن as a gerundial/locative phrase ‘in/by walking’ and تذرو (3rd‑person verb) means ‘she scatters/disperses’ — i.e. ‘when she walks she scatters (beauty/charm).’
segments.s071-g111: چنین داد پاسخ is a concise narrative clause: چنین ‘thus’ + داد (past ‘gave’) + پاسخ ‘an answer’ — i.e. ‘thus he answered.’ The following که introduces the content clause (that …) with پیمان من marking the topic/possessive ‘my covenant/oath.’
segments.s071-g112: درست است is a verbless/predicative construction ‘is firm/true.’ اگر here introduces a concessive condition (‘even if’/‘if’), and بگسلد جان من is subordinate: بگسلد (subjunctive/present subjunctive form of گسستن used like ‘should break/if it breaks’) + جان من ‘my life,’ expressing ‘even if it should break my life’ — idiomatically ‘even if it costs my life.’
segments.s071-g97: همی is an adverb of continuity/emphasis placed before the clause ('still/continually'); the core clause is بر تو بر خواندیم آفرین where بر ... بر + خواندیم is a light-prepositional framing meaning 'directed to/at you we read/sang/called'; خواندیم is perfect/past first-person plural 'we recited/sang/called out' and آفرین is the exclamation/praise object of خواندیم. Word order is poetic (preposed همی and the doubled preposition بر for emphasis).
segments.s071-g98: همان 'that same' picks up the previously mentioned acclamation; بر here marks the target ('upon/to') جهاندار شاه زمین is a stacked nominal phrase ('sovereign — king of the earth') with no ezâfe explicitly written (poetic compression). The whole segment says that the same praise was also directed at the world‑ruler, the king of the earth.
segments.s071-g99: کنون آمدم is a straightforward perfective clause 'now I have come'; تا introduces purpose or the object of coming ('in order that' / 'to find out') and is followed by the interrogative clause هوای تو چیست where هوای 'your wish/desire' is the subject and چیست is the literary present copula 'is/what is'. The whole gives: 'I came to ask/learn what your desire is.'
segments.s071-g100: ز کابل ترا places the speech-location/point of reference ('from Kabul' or 'as regards Kabul', with ترا functioning as a dative 'for you'); دشمن و دوست is the coordinate predicate 'enemy and friend' and کی ست is the interrogative copula 'who is'. Word order is topical (place/dative first) then the question 'who is (your) enemy and (your) friend?'.
segments.s071-g101: اگر introduces a protasis 'if'; ما is the subject 'we' and two predicate adjectives follow: گنهکار 'sinners' and بدگوهریم where بدگوهر is an adjective/noun 'ill‑natured, worthless' and -یم is the 1st‑person plural copula 'we are'. In classical Persian it is common to attach the copular ending only to the final predicate, so both predicates are read as describing 'we'.
segments.s071-g102: بدین is the classical compound corresponding to modern به این 'to/for this' (here marking 'this kingship'); پادشاهی 'kingship/realm' is the noun; نه اندر خوریم is a negated phrase meaning 'we do not enter/fit/belong' — خوریم is first‑person plural and here idiomatically means 'to befit/adhere to/participate in' (an older usage of خوردن). The whole clause denies that the speakers belong in this kingship.
segments.s071-g103: من اینک gives the subject with a temporal adverb 'I, now'; به پیش توام is a prepositional phrase 'in your presence/ before you' where توام is the fused pronoun+copula ('I am at/with you' in classical style) and مستمند is the predicate adjective 'needy/poor'. The order is subject–time–locative–predicate, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s071-g104: بکش‌گر is a vocative or epithet 'O slayer/persecutor'; the sequence کشی (here) functions as the verb form 'if you kill' (the protasis), with archaic conditional particle ور 'if' introducing an alternative clause; ببندی is the potential/conditional 'if you bind' and ببند is the plain imperative 'bind' — the line offers two conditional possibilities (kill or bind) followed by a curt imperative, typical of direct, compressed address in epic verse.
segments.s071-g41: گرفته is a participial/past form meaning 'having taken' or 'taken' that precedes the noun phrase. یکی جام هر یک به دست is literal: 'one cup — each in (his) hand' (Persian often strings quantifier + noun + distributive phrase). به دست here marks that the cup is held 'into/onto the hand' (i.e. 'in the hand').
segments.s071-g42: بفرمود is an honorific/past narrative verb 'he ordered/said'. It governs the following clause; کامد is an archaic/formal verb-form meaning 'come' (here understood as 'that they come' or 'they came'), and به جای نشست is a small clause 'to the place sat' meaning 'sat in their places' — together: 'he ordered (that) they come and sit in their places.'
segments.s071-g43: به پیش سپهبد is a prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of the spahbed' (پیش = 'before/in front of/in the presence of'). فرو ریختند is a compound perfect/past 'they poured down' used idiomatically for 'they rushed down/forward' (فرو = downward + ریختند = past of 'to pour/flow').
segments.s071-g44: همه یک به دیگر is a distributive/reciprocal phrase 'all, one to another' and برآمیختند is past tense 'they mingled/merged/merged together' — a simple past narrative describing them joining together.
segments.s071-g45: چو = 'when'. با پهلوان marks the party involved 'with the hero'. کار بر ساختند is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'they brought the matter to an end / settled the affair' (کار = affair/matter; بر ساختند = completed/resolved).
segments.s071-g46: ز + noun phrase is the old poetic preposition 'from/of'. بیگانه خانه is 'the stranger's house' (genitive relation implied). بپرداختند here is an archaic past verb meaning 'they set off / departed / left' (common in older Persian in contexts like 'to set out / go away'); the sense in modern lists may not show this meaning, so the precise lexical sense is idiomatic/archaic 'left'.
segments.s071-g47: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). The word order places the verb next to the speaker's name: 'چنین گفت سیندخت' = 'Sindokht said thus'. با پهلوان here means 'to the hero' (with + person used in the sense 'in the presence of / to').
segments.s071-g48: که introduces a reported/content clause 'that...'. با رای تو = 'by/with your counsel' where رای means 'plan/counsel' (رای = advice/counsel). گردد is the classical/present-subjunctive/predicative form of 'become' (here 'will become' / 'becomes'), so پیر گردد جوان literally 'the old becomes young' — Persian allows this inversion (adjective/noun order) for poetic effect.
segments.s071-g9: که is the conjunction 'that' introducing the reported clause. آمد is a simple past 3rd‑singular 'came'. فرستاده‌ای uses the participial/derived noun 'the sent one' with the -ای/-i indefinite/partitive flavour — here functioning as 'an envoy' or 'messenger'. کابلی is an adjectival epithet postposed to the noun ('Kabul‑born' / 'of Kabul'), so the whole phrase reads 'that a Kabul envoy came'.
segments.s071-g10: به نزد is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence of / before'. سپهبد is a title (spahbed); یل is a descriptive appositive 'hero' and زابلی is an adjective/nisba 'of Zabul' attached postpositively — together 'the spahbed, the hero of Zabul'.
segments.s071-g11: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'. مهراب here is a proper name (Mehrab) — the provided lexical senses do not list the personal name, so it is treated as a name source. گرد آوریده is a compound participle meaning 'gathered/collected' (گرد + آوریده), and پیام is the head noun 'message' — literally 'a message gathered from Mehrab'.
segments.s071-g12: به نزد again marks motion/taking something 'to the presence of'. سپهبد is the title; جهانگیر is used as a personal name/epithet (here the name Jahangir), and سام is the personal name Sam — 'to the spahbed Jahangir Sam'.
segments.s071-g13: بیامد is past 'came' (poetic form with the prefix bi‑). بر plus a following name marks direction/toward ('to Sam'). یل پرده‌دار is an appositive/title cluster: یل 'hero' + پرده‌دار 'keeper of the curtain / chamberlain', describing Sam.
segments.s071-g14: بگفت is 'he said' and و simply coordinates; بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here introduces the result/purpose 'that/so that', and داد بار is literal 'give the load' — idiomatically 'deliver/pay the tribute/load' with داد as past of 'to give' and بار 'load/tribute'.
segments.s071-g15: فرود آمد is a compound verb 'alighted / dismounted' (فرود + آمد). از marks origin 'from', and اسپ سیندخت is a possessive phrase 'the steed of Sindokht' (poetic ordering without an explicit ezāfe). و رفت simply links 'and went' (رفت = 'he went').
segments.s071-g16: به پیش = 'into the presence of / before'. سپهبد is the title. The verb خرامید ('strode proudly') appears before its subject (a common poetic inversion), and تفت is the subject proper name (here treated as a name rather than the lexical verb 'to sauté' listed in the lexical senses), so the clause reads 'strode proudly before the spahbed Taft.'
segments.s071-g49: This is a simple past clause: subject «بزرگان» (the great/elders) + prepositional source «ز تو» ('from you') + object «دانش» and verb «آموختند» (past, 3rd pl.). In classical Persian آموختن here means 'to learn' (not 'to teach'), so the clause reads 'the great men learned knowledge from you.'
segments.s071-g50: Another past clause with fronted dative/benefactive «به تو» ('for/ because of you') followed by the object «تیرگیها» (pl. 'darknesses/glooms') and the past verb «برافروختند» (they kindled/they lit). Note the plural verb agrees with an implied plural agent (same 'great men').
segments.s071-g51: An agentless/resultative passive-like construction: the instrumental/adverbial phrase «به مهر تو» ('by/through your affection,favour') precedes the predication «دست بدی شد بسته». Here «شد» (became) + adjective «بسته» expresses a change of state ('was/ became bound'), and «دست» means 'power/hand' (i.e. the agency of evil).
segments.s071-g52: This clause parallels the previous one and omits an explicit verb (the copular/auxiliary 'شد' from the prior clause is understood). «به گرزت» is instrumental ('by/with your mace' — گرز + enclitic -ت), and the predicate «گشاده ره ایزدی» places the adjective before the noun for poetic effect: 'a wide (گشاده) way (ره) divine (ایزدی)' — i.e. 'the divine way was made wide/open.'
segments.s071-g53: The compound «گنهکار‌گر» uses the conditional/ concessive element «-گر» (if/when) attached to 'گنهکار', so the line reads roughly 'If there was a sinner...'. The repeated «بود» marks two short predicates: first the conditional clause 'اگر گنهکار بود' and then 'مهراب بود' ('there was a mihrab/sanctuary').
segments.s071-g54: This is a cause/result clause: preposition «ز» ('from/because of') + «خون دلش» ('the blood of his heart' — خون + دل + possessive suffix ش) gives the source, then «دیده سیراب بود» ('the eye was sated/quenched'). «سیراب بود» is a stative past ('was satisfied/filled').
segments.s071-g55: Interrogative fronting «چه» ('what') + verb «کرد» (past 'did') yields 'what did Kabul do...'. The phrase «سر بیگناهان» is an object phrase ('the heads of the innocent'), with the place-name «کابل» as the subject placed between object and verb (poetic inversion is common).
segments.s071-g56: Two short predicates: «کجا اندر آورد» is an interrogative subordinate 'where did (he) bring (them)?' («آورد» = 'bring'); the following «باید بگرد» uses the impersonal/modal «باید» ('one must') + present-subjunctive/imperative «بگرد» ('search/turn about'), giving obligation 'one must search (to find out)'.
segments.s071-g89: زمین را ببوسید و بر پای خاست is two coordinated actions. زمین را uses the object marker را after the noun (object-before-verb order is normal in Persian). ببوسید is a past/simple perfective verb ‘he kissed’ (classical past form). و links the two verbs, and بر پای خاست is an idiomatic phrase: بر + پای (on/to the foot) + خاست (past of خاستن, ‘to rise/stand up’) = ‘rose to his feet’. Word order is noun + object marker + verb, then conjunction + locative phrase + past verb.
segments.s071-g90: بگفت is a classical past form ‘he said’. آنچه is a relative/indefinite pronoun ‘that which/whatever’. اندر نهان بود is a locative + adjective + copula (‘that which was hidden’), where اندر = ‘in/within’, نهان = ‘hidden’, and بود is the past copula ‘was’. راست is a predicate adjective ‘true’ placed after the copula (typical Persian predicate order).
segments.s071-g91: که introduces the content clause (‘that’). من خویش is emphatic ‘I myself’ (خویش = self/own; here reflexive/emphatic). ضحاکم contains the name ضحاک plus the archaic 1st‑person copular/clitic -م, so ضحاکم = ‘I am Zahhak’ (classical Persian often drops an explicit verb, using the suffix). ای پهلوان is a vocative addressing the hero (‘O champion’).
segments.s071-g92: زن گرد مهراب روشن‌روان is an appositive/descriptive phrase. زن = ‘woman’. گرد is the old/post‑classical preposition/adverb ‘around, about’ giving a locative sense (‘the woman around Mehrab’). مهراب here functions as a proper name/place (Mehrab), and روشن‌روان is an epithet ‘bright‑souled’ placed after the noun as a descriptive adjective. The whole phrase further identifies the woman referred to.
segments.s071-g93: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic ‘the same’ or ‘that very’. مام is a dialectal/poetic word for ‘mother’. رودابه is the proper name Rudabeh. ماه روی is a fixed epithet: ماه = ‘moon’ used metaphorically for beauty and روی = ‘face’, so ماه‌روی = ‘moon‑faced’ (beautiful‑faced), placed after the name as an epithet.
segments.s071-g94: که introduces a relative clause ‘who/which’. دستان is the plural of دست ‘hands’ (here literal). همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker (often used in epic narration to indicate ongoing or repeated action: ‘continually/now’). جان is the object ‘life/soul’, and فشاند is a present/habitual form ‘(she) scatters/sprinkles’. بروی here functions as a directional/locative element ‘upon the face’ (b- + روی), but the supplied lemma/senses list corresponds to a different verb-form, so the listed senses do not include this locative use (see senses field).
segments.s071-g95: همه دودمان = ‘all the lineage / the whole family/dynasty’. پیش یزدان پاک = ‘before the Pure God’ where پیش here means ‘in the presence of / before (in front of)’, یزدان = God, and پاک = ‘pure’ as an epithet following the divine name. This is a customary blessing/invocation construction.
segments.s071-g96: شب تیره = ‘dark night’ (noun + adjective). تا introduces a temporal/until clause (‘until’). برکشد is a classical form meaning ‘(he/it) lift(s)/raise(s)’ (ب‑ prefix here is classical and the verb expresses causation or removal), and روز چاک is an image of the day made visible/open (چاک functions as a descriptive word meaning opened/cleared). The clause means roughly ‘until the dark night is lifted and the day appears/revealed’.
segments.s071-g121: چنان آفریند is an evaluative past/action: چنان 'so' + آفریند (classical past 'he made/arranged'). که introduces a result clause ('so that') and آیدش is 'comes to him' with enclitic -ش ('to him/his'); رای ('counsel/plan') is the thing that 'comes' — literal order is verb+enclitic then noun (آیدش رای) typical of older poetic word order.
segments.s071-g122: نمانیم is the negative of مانیم and here functions idiomatically as 'we could not remain' (classical narrative negative); و links the clauses and ماندیم is the simple past 'we remained' used here to report the resulting state. با‌های‌های is an onomatopoetic adverbial phrase 'with loud sobs/crying' describing how they remained.
segments.s071-g123: This is a balanced coordinate pair introduced by یکی ... و یکی ... ('one/one and one/one'): بر فراز = 'on the summit/high' (preposition بر + noun فراز), and در نشیب = 'in the descent/low place' (preposition در + noun نشیب). The structure contrasts positions by parallel prepositional phrases.
segments.s071-g124: Another parallel pair: یکی با فزونی یکی با نهیب — 'one with increase/abundance, one with rebuke'. با is the preposition 'with' linking the abstract nouns فزونی (increase/plenty) and نهیب (rebuke/thunderous cry) in parallel contrast; word order repeats یکی ... یکی for rhythm and contrast.
segments.s071-g125: یکی begins an indefinite clause 'one (person)'. از فزایش is a prepositional phrase ('from/out of increase/abundance') that modifies the following nominal predicate. دل آراسته is a verbless nominal phrase in which دل is the patient/possessed noun and آراسته (a past‑participle used adjectivally) predicates that the heart is 'adorned' — Persian often omits a copula here.
segments.s071-g126: ز کمی is the poetic variant of از کمی, a prepositional phrase 'from/through lack' that sets the cause. دل دیگری کاسته is structured as 'the other one's heart (دل دیگری) [is] diminished (کاسته)' — کاسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate (copula omitted) meaning 'made smaller/diminished'. Word order is noun + modifier (دیگری) + predicate, as is common in Persian.
segments.s071-g127: یکی ('one' / 'someone') is the subject. نامه is the head noun 'a letter'. با + noun ('با لابه دردمند') is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with [a] lamenting/sorrowful entreaty' that modifies نامه; دردمند is an adjective 'sorrowful/in pain' qualifying لابه. Note that لابه here is a poetic 'entreaty/plea' construction attached to نامه.
segments.s071-g128: نبشتم is a 1st‑person singular past form (–م ending) of an archaic/rare verb (lemma given as 'نب'); in context it functions as the past action 'sent/wrote/placed' the letter (Ferdowsi uses many rare/archaic verb forms). به نزدیک شاه بلند is a goal phrase: به + نزدیک ('to/into the near presence of') followed by the noun phrase شاه بلند ('the lofty/tall king'); word order is preposition + noun phrase.
segments.s071-g73: یکی (one/a certain) opens the clause as an indefinite numeral/quantifier; سخت (hard/severe) modifies پیمانت (pact/pledge) which carries the 2nd‑person enclitic ت (= “your”); پیمانت خواهم is an idiomatic object + verb order where خواهم (present/future of خواستن) means “I will demand/ask (for)”; نخست is an adverb (’first’) placed after the verb for emphasis in poetic word order.
segments.s071-g74: که introduces a purpose/result clause here (’so that’); لرزان is an adjective/participle ’trembling/shaken’ used predicatively; شود is the present/subjunctive form of شدن meaning ‘may become / may be (shaken)’; ز (poetic form of از) marks source/agent here (‘by/from him’); the sequence بر و بوم و رست lists places/holdings (bar, bōm, and rīst/rast) and is co‑ordinated by و; overall the clause gives the intended result of the pledge.
segments.s071-g75: که here introduces the subordinate clause expressing a wish/negative result (’that/so that’); از تو (from you) locates the source of harm; نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive of آمدن (’may not come’ / ’does not come’); به + جانم marks the affected good (’to my life/my person’), with جان the noun and the enclitic م implicitly marking possession; گزند is the noun ‘harm’ and is the clause’s head.
segments.s071-g76: نه is the negation/contrastive particle cancelling the following description; آنکس که = ’that person who/whom’ introduces a relative clause; بر من بود is a verbal phrase where بر + personal object (من) + بود (past copula) expresses relation/treatment toward me (’was to/for me’); ارجمند is a predicate adjective meaning ‘esteemed/precious’; the order (بود ارجمند) is poetic inversion, with the copula kept before the adjective.
segments.s071-g77: مرا is the dative/topic form meaning ‘to me/for me’ (classical Persian idiom = ‘I have’); کاخ و ایوان are coordinated nouns ‘palace and iwan/porch’; آباد is a postpositive adjective meaning ‘prosperous/inhabited’ modifying the preceding nouns; هست is the copula placed at the end — literal order: ‘To me palace and iwan prosperous there is’ (i.e. ‘I have a prosperous palace and iwan’).
segments.s071-g78: همان here functions as ‘the same’ / ‘likewise’; گنج و خویشان و بنیاد is a coordinated list (treasure, relatives, and foundation/endowment) and هست is again the final copula ‘there is/exists’ — the clause states additional possessions or establishments in the same register.
segments.s071-g79: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’; ایمن شوم = subjunctive/potential ‘I become safe’ (شوم = ’may I become’ / ’I become’); هر چه = ‘whatever/anything that’; گویی is a present/subjunctive form (’you say’ / ’that you say’); بگوی is an imperative or jussive form (poetically echoing ‘say it’); the clause thus says ‘when I am safe, say whatever you will — say it’.
segments.s071-g80: بگویم is first‑person present/future/jussive ‘I will say / I may say’; بجویم is first‑person present/subjunctive of جستن ‘I will seek/search for’; بدین is the instrumental phrase ‘with this’ (ب- + دین/این in older spelling) taking the following noun phrase; آب روی is a poetic compound ‘water‑face’ = ‘fair/clear face’ used as an adverbial/instrumental image (’with this fair countenance’); together: ‘I will say and I will seek it with this fair face.’
segments.s071-g161: بیامد is a past‑tense verb 'he came'. بر + اسم (سام) marks motion/position toward or before Sam ('came to/ before Sam'). بردش is برد + the clitic ش (third‑person object) and here functions transitively ('he led/took for him'); نماز is the direct object 'prayer' — the whole phrase means he came to Sam and performed/led the prayer for him. Word order is Verb + prepositional phrase + conjunction + verb+object, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s071-g162: سخن گفت is a light‑verb construction: سخن (speech, words) + گفت (he said/spoke) = 'he spoke'. بااو is the fused preposition + pronoun 'with him' (با او), and زمانی دراز is an adverbial phrase where زمانی ('a (length of) time' used adverbially) + دراز ('long') = 'for a long time'. The clause therefore means 'he spoke with him for a long while.'
segments.s071-g163: به دستوری is an adverbial phrase where به marks manner ('by') and دستوری ('by/according to an order' or 'commanding') specifies the cause/manner. بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return' used here verbally in the narrative sense ('he returned'). به جای is a destination phrase 'to the place/position' (here 'to his post').
segments.s071-g164: شدن here means 'to become' or more loosely 'to set out / came to be' with an implied subject ('he became / was'). شادمان is a predicate/adverbial 'glad, rejoicing'. سوی کابل is a directional phrase 'toward Kabul'. خدای at the end is an exclamatory vocative ('O God!') expressing emotion or emphasis. Together they read as 'glad, he went toward Kabul — O God!'
segments.s071-g165: دگر here functions adverbially as 'again/anew' or 'otherwise'. ساختن is the infinitive/verb 'to make/arrange', and کار مهمان نو is a noun phrase 'the guest's affair(s) new' (کار مهمان = the guest's business; نو postpositively qualifies it as 'new' or 'anew'). The segment means he rearranged/renewed the guest's affairs.
segments.s071-g166: نمودن is a light verb meaning 'to make/do/show' and here introduces the second action. به marks the indirect object 'to/for', داماد is 'the groom', and پیمان نو is the direct object 'a new pact/covenant'. The whole segment means 'he made (or gave) a new covenant to the groom.'
segments.s071-g167: ورا is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (و + را fused here), directing the speech to someone. سام یل is apposition 'Sam, the hero.' گفت is the past verb 'said.' Then two imperatives follow: برگرد ('return/come back!') and رو (imperative of رفتن/رَوَـ meaning 'go/turn' or 'face'); the sequence gives a pair of commands addressed to the addressee.
segments.s071-g168: بگو is the imperative 'say/tell'. آنچه دیدی is a relative clause 'what you saw' (آنچه = 'that which/what' + دیدی = 'you saw'). به مهراب گو: به marks the indirect object 'to Mehrab' (here Mehrab is a person — a proper name), and the final گو is an emphatic/echoing imperative 'tell' appended for force ('go tell Mehrab'). The whole is 'Tell Mehrab whatever you saw.'
segments.s071-g137: عروس ار = a topical subject + archaic conditional: عروس (bride/maiden) + ار (classical conditional particle, equivalent to اگر ‘if’). به مهر اندرون is an adverbial phrase: به مهر (with love/affection) + اندرون (inside/inwardly). همچو اوست is the predicate: همچو (like/so) + او (him/that) + ست (poetic/copular form of است ‘is’). The whole segment therefore says ‘if the bride inwardly loves/is such toward him.’
segments.s071-g138: سزد‌گر برآیند هر دو ز پوست is an impersonal/result clause: سزدگر (fitting/appropriate) functions as an evaluative predicate (‘it is fitting’). برآیند here is a classical/formal verbal form meaning ‘(they) come forth / should come forth’ (subjunctive/optative sense in context). هر دو = ‘both’, and ز پوست = ‘from the skin’ (ز is the poetic form of از ‘from’). Together: ‘it is fitting that both come out of their skins.’
segments.s071-g139: یکی روی آن بچه اژدها: یکی (‘one’ / ‘a certain’) qualifies روی (‘face’ / appearance) of آن بچه اژدها (‘that baby dragon’). Word order is noun phrase + modifier: ‘one face/appearance of that young dragon.’
segments.s071-g140: مرا نیز بنمای و بستان بها: مرا (me as definite object; here split in the listing as م + را) + نیز (‘also’) + بنمای (imperative/optative ‘show!’, classical form of نماییدن) + و (‘and’) + بستان بها. In context بستان بها functions as a command/request ‘take the fee/receive the price’ (بها = price). The segment therefore asks to be shown as well and instructs to take the payment.
segments.s071-g141: بدو گفت سیندخت اگر پهلوان is a reporting clause plus the conditional introducer: بدو (‘to him’) is an indirect‑object prepositional phrase, گفت (here the past verb ‘said’) introduces speech, سیندخت is the speaker’s name. اگر پهلوان = ‘if (you) are/act as a champion/hero’ — اگر is the conditional particle and پهلوان is the predicate noun.
segments.s071-g142: کند بنده را شاد و روشن‌روان: کند is the (classical) present/subjunctive form of کردن ‘make/do’ (here ‘make’); بنده را is the patient/definite object (‘me’ used as a humble self‑reference in classical speech); شاد و روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives (‘happy and light‑souled/cheerful’). The clause therefore is an imperative/optative: ‘make me happy and bright‑spirited.’
segments.s071-g143: چماند به کاخ من اندر سمند: چماند is a classical/past verb meaning 'so/thus he remained or stayed' (چو ماند). The locative phrase places him ‘in my palace’ (به کاخ من اندر, a poetic stacking of prepositions) and سمند (‘steed’) indicates he is on horseback. Word order is poetic and slightly inverted compared with modern colloquial speech.
segments.s071-g144: سرم بر شود به آسمان بلند: سرم (‘my head’) + بر شود (بر as directional/privative prefix + شود ‘become’ → ‘is lifted/raised’) + به آسمان بلند (‘to the high sky/heavens’). The construction expresses exaltation: ‘my head was uplifted to the lofty heavens.’
segments.s071-g169: سزاوار is an adjective ('fitting, appropriate') placed before the person (او) for emphasis; او is the 3rd‑person pronoun referring to the recipient. خلعت is the noun 'robe (of honour)' and آراستند is the 3rd‑person plural past verb 'they adorned/dressed' — the typical Persian word order here is adjective + indirect/object + direct object + past verb, so the clause means 'they adorned him with a suitable robe.'
segments.s071-g170: ز گنج is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from (the) treasure'; آنچه is the relative/indefinite 'that which / whatever'; پرمایه‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more substantial / more valuable'; خواستند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they wanted/desired' — together: 'from the treasury, whatever more valuable they wanted.'
segments.s071-g171: بِکابل is the prepositional phrase 'to Kabul' (بِ- as older/poetic 'to'); دگر here means 'again / back' or 'besides' (contextually 'back'); سام را uses the object marker را to mark Sam as the one affected; هر چه بود is a common idiom 'all that there was' (هر = every/each, چه = whatever, بود = was). The implied verb ('they sent' or 'they returned') is recovered from the surrounding narrative.
segments.s071-g172: Repeated ز is the poetic short form of 'from/with' introducing a list: کاخ (palace), زباغ and زکشت are orthographic compounds (ز + باغ, ز + کشت) meaning 'and gardens' and 'and cultivated fields' respectively, and درود here names honors/blessings/tributes. This is a catalog of what accompanied or belonged to him.
segments.s071-g173: دگر here means 'other' or 'remaining'; چارپایان is the plural noun 'four‑legged animals, quadrupeds'; دوشیدنی is an adjectival deverbal form meaning 'to be milked / milkable' (the -یدنی suffix makes a potential/usable adjective). The phrase lists animals intended for milking.
segments.s071-g174: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; گستردنی is an adjective 'that can be spread' (spreadable, e.g. carpets or cloths); هم adds 'also'; the second ز introduces پوشیدنی, 'that can be worn' (wearable garments). The segment continues the catalogue of goods by function (to be spread, to be worn).
segments.s071-g175: به + proper name (به سیندخت) marks the recipient 'to/for Sindokht'; بخشید here is the past verb 'gave, bestowed' (in older texts it can be 3rd‑singular or plural depending on subject); و links clauses; دستش is دست + enclitic ش 'her hand'; بدست here means 'into the hand / in the hand' and forms the idiom completed by the following verb (گرفت) meaning 'took her hand in his hand.'
segments.s071-g176: گرفت is the past 'he took / he seized' completing the idiom 'دستش بدست گرفت' (took her hand). و is 'and'; یکی here means 'one (person) / someone' (used to indicate another party); نیز 'also'; پیمان is 'covenant/pledge' and ببست (literally 'he bound') is the past verb forming the idiom 'پیمان بستن' = 'to make a pact/oath.'
segments.s071-g129: This is an archaic order: prepositional phrase + verb + subject. به نزد = 'to the presence of'; شد is the past/preterite auxiliary/verb meaning 'happened/arrived' placed before the subject; زال زر is the subject phrase ('Zal' with زر 'gold' as accompaniment, i.e. 'Zal with gold').
segments.s071-g130: چنان شد = 'it became/it came to pass thus'. که introduces the following clause ('that' / 'so that'). گفتی here functions as a noun-ish use ('the saying' / 'as is said'/'as you said') rather than a simple verb form. برآورده is a past‑participle/adjectival use meaning 'raised/put up' (here describing پر); پر = 'feather/plume'.
segments.s071-g131: به زین اندر آمد = 'he came onto/into the saddle' (به ... اندر آمد is an older way of saying 'he mounted'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'which/so that' and زین را ندید is a direct object clause 'he did not see the saddle' (را marks زین as definite object).
segments.s071-g132: همان = 'the very/that' (emphatic); نعل اسپش = 'the horseshoe of his steed' (اسپش = 'his steed/horse', the -ش is possessive); زمین را ندید = 'he did not see the ground' / idiomatically 'did not see the shoe touch the ground.' را again marks the definite object 'زمین'.
segments.s071-g133: بدین is an idiomatic fused form meaning 'thereupon/with this' (here segmented in the dataset as ب + دین); زال را is the definite object marked by را ('to/for Zal' as the addressee of the reply). شاه is the subject 'the king', and پاسخ دهد is the verb phrase 'gives an answer' (دهد = 'gives/utter[s]'; in epic prose the present/subjunctive form often carries narrative force).
segments.s071-g134: چو خندان شود = 'when he becomes/smiles' (چو = 'when'; خندان = 'smiling'; شود = 'becomes/is'); رای فرخ نهد = 'he sets down/offers a fortunate decision/plan' where رای = 'opinion/plan/decision', فرخ = 'fortunate/happy' (adjective modifying رای), and نهد (نهاد) = 'places/sets forth' (third person).
segments.s071-g135: که introduces an explanatory clause 'that'. پرورده مرغ = 'a bird that was reared/raised' (پرورده = 'reared/nurtured' used adjectivally before مرغ = 'bird'). بی‌دل = 'without heart' i.e. 'heartless/ungrateful'. شدست is an archaic/perfect form meaning 'has become' or 'it became'.
segments.s071-g136: از آب مژه = 'from the water of the eyelash' (a poetic phrase for 'from tears'); پای در گل شدست = 'its foot came to be in the mud' (پای = 'foot'; در = 'in'; گل = 'mud/clay'; شدست = 'has become' / 'was become'). The line pictures decline: despite being reared, the bird ends with its foot stuck in the mud because of its tears.
segments.s071-g153: This pre‑verbal nominal/temporal cluster sets the scene: من is the first‑person subject 'I'; اینک is an adverb 'now'; پس is a temporal connective 'after/thereafter'; نامه اندر دمان is a noun phrase with اندر 'in/within' governing دمان 'hem/skirt' — 'the letter in (my) hem.' The whole segment supplies the time and object that the following verb phrase will refer to.
segments.s071-g154: Two verbal items follow. بیایم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form used narratively as a future/intent 'I will/shall come.' نجویم here contains the negative prefix ن‑ plus the subjunctive/root جویم, so it means 'I do not (or will not) seek.' The final prepositional phrase به ره بر زمان ('toward the way / at that time') is a temporal/locational adjunct: به ره = 'by/on the road', and بر زمان = 'for/at the time.'
segments.s071-g155: دوم روز is a temporal noun phrase 'the second day' (adjective before noun). چون acts as a conjunction 'when/as' introducing the time clause. چشمه آفتاب is a poetic compound 'the spring/fountain of the sun' (a sunrise metaphor) serving as the subject of the clause.
segments.s071-g156: بجنبید is a past/preterite (3rd person) verb 'stirred/moved' agreeing with the preceding subject; و is simply 'and.' بیدار here is an adjective 'awake,' and شد is the auxiliary/past 'became' used to form 'became awake' (= awoke). سر ز خواب is an idiomatic particle phrase 'head from sleep' — 'he raised his head from sleep' i.e., awoke.
segments.s071-g157: This is a simple subject‑verb‑object cluster. گرانمایه is an epithet 'precious/esteemed' modifying the proper name سیندخت. بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'to set/put' used here causatively/'set' as 'she turned/placed', and روی is the noun 'face' — 'she set/turned her face.'
segments.s071-g158: This is a directional phrase: به درگاه = 'toward the threshold/court.' سالار دیهیم is a compound title 'the lord/chief of the countryside' (سالار + دیهیم 'rural'). جوی here is a participial/active adjective 'seeking' (literally 'in search of'), so the whole phrase says she turned her face toward the court in search of the rural chief.
segments.s071-g159: روارو برآمد is a fixed verbal/adverbial combination meaning 'came forward/stepped forth' (روارو 'face‑to‑face/opposite' used adverbially + برآمد 'emerged/rose'). ز درگاه سام is the source phrase 'from Sam's threshold/doorway.'
segments.s071-g160: مه بانوان is an honorific phrase 'the great/noble ladies' (مه here = 'great'). خواندندش is past 3rd‑plural خواندند 'they called/read' plus the object clitic ‑ش 'her/him', so 'they called her.' به نام is the prepositional phrase 'by (her) name' — 'they called her by name.'
segments.s071-g177: پذیرفت is a simple past 3rd-person verb meaning “accepted.” مر is an archaic possessive/dative form meaning “my”/“to me,” here attached before دخت (‘daughter’) to yield “my daughter.” او را is the definite-object sequence “her” (او) + object marker را; word order is verb — possessor + noun — pronominal object marker + name (بزال) used apposition/subject after the object in this poetic, somewhat inverted order.
segments.s071-g178: که introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that/that”), and باشند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive (here optative/subjunctive) meaning “may they be / might they be.” هر دو literally “each two” functions as “both.” بشادی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase “in joy / joyfully.” همال is a noun used figuratively here as “companions/peers” (literally bearer/porter but used as ‘fellow’).
segments.s071-g179: سرافراز is an adjective “honored/proud.” گردی here is an archaic/verbal element attached to the adjective to mark becoming or an optative sense (literal formation equivalent to ‘become honored’ or a blessing ‘may you be honored’); it is an old poetic usage rather than the noun meanings listed. مردی دویست is an inverted quantitative phrase: مردی (man/men) + دویست (two hundred) = “two hundred men.” The phrase order (adjective/verb phrase then quantity) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s071-g180: بدو is the clitic phrase “to him” (به + او contracted), and داد is past “gave.” و links clauses. گفتش is “said to him” (گفت + enclitic ش = to him). که introduces the reported content/quote. ایدر means “here,” and مایست is an archaic/palatalized predicative form: in context it functions like ‘is for you’ / ‘this is your place/portion’ — an old or dialectal phrase used as a locative/predicative comment.
segments.s071-g181: به + place (کابل) marks destination/location: “in/at Kabul.” بباش is an old imperative form “be/remain” (poetic imperative of بودن), and بمان is the imperative “stay/remain.” The coordinating و links the two imperatives; به شادی is an adverbial “in joy” describing the manner of remaining.
segments.s071-g182: ازین پس is a fixed temporal phrase “from now on / henceforth” (از + این + پس). مترس is the negative imperative “fear not” (م + ترس = do not fear). از بد بدگمان is a brief object/compliment: از بد “from/of bad (things)” and بدگمان “suspicion/ill‑will” — together instructing the addressee not to be afraid of ill‑omens or malicious suspicion.
segments.s071-g183: شگفته شد is a compound of the participle شگفته (“bloomed/enraptured/brightened”) plus the auxiliary شد (“became”), so together “became bright/bloomed” or “was revived.” آن روی is “that face,” پژمرده qualifies روی as “withered/pale,” and ماه here is a simile/metaphor: “moon,” often used poetically for a beautiful or luminous face.
segments.s071-g184: به + نیک اختری = under/by an auspicious star (نیک = auspicious/good; اختری = star‑like/stellar; the combination is a poetic locative/agent phrase). برگرفتند is past 3rd‑person plural “they took up / set forth on” (برگرفتن = to take up / adopt / set out), and راه is the direct object “road / way.” The whole clause means they set out on the path under a favorable star/omen.
segments.s071-g145: This clause places the prepositional phrase 'به کابل' ('to Kabul') before the noun phrase 'چنو شهریار' ('such a king'), with the verb 'آوریم' at the end. 'چنو' is a demonstrative/adjectival particle meaning 'such' that directly precedes the noun. 'آوریم' is the 1st‑person plural verbal form used in epic narrative here as a past/actional verb 'we brought'.
segments.s071-g146: Subject 'همه' ('all/everyone') opens the clause; 'پیش او' is a prepositional phrase ('in the presence of / before him'); 'جان‌نثار' is a noun meaning those who would give their lives (life‑devotees) and functions as the object; 'آوریم' repeats the first‑person plural verb 'we brought/offered' (used as past). The clause therefore means 'all (of us) before him brought/offered ourselves as life‑devotees.'
segments.s071-g147: Word order is poetic/elliptical: 'لب سام سیندخت پرخنده دید' places the locative/possession cluster and the name before the predicate. 'لب' ('lip / on the lip of') is used idiomatically (with the possessive relation understood despite the missing ezâfe) to indicate a smiling expression; 'سیندخت' is the proper name; 'پرخنده' is an adjectival compound 'full of laughter / smiling'; 'دید' is 3rd‑person singular past 'he saw'. Poetic omission of the ezâfe and subject is typical here.
segments.s071-g148: 'همه بیخ کین از دلش کنده دید' is another clause with participial structure: 'همه بیخ کین' ('the whole root of hatred') is the object phrase, 'از دلش' ('out of her heart') gives the source (with 'ش' as the possessive suffix = her), 'کنده' is a past participle 'uprooted/torn out', and 'دید' ('he saw') is the reporting verb. The construction expresses that he perceived the complete removal (uprooting) of hatred from her heart; poetry often omits relative markers like 'که'.
segments.s071-g149: Object phrase placed before the verb: 'نوندی دلاور' is a proper/epithetic name phrase (a named/rare term + 'دلاور' = 'the valiant Navandi/valiant one'), followed by the adverbial simile 'به کردار باد' ('in the manner/behavior of the wind'). 'به' functions adverbially here ('in/with'), 'کردار' = 'deed/behaviour', and 'باد' = 'wind'. Together they modify the action that follows in the next clause.
segments.s071-g150: 'برافگند' is a 3rd‑person past (he/they cast/threw down, scattered). The conjunction 'و' links the second action. 'مهراب را' is a marked object with 'را' (here Mehrab is the recipient/person being told), 'مژده' = 'good news/tidings' is the object of 'داد', and 'داد' is the past 'gave'. The subject (he) is omitted as is normal; the line says he overthrew/shot away the valiant one and gave Mehrab the news.
segments.s071-g151: 'کز' here functions as 'from / because of' (poetic variant of 'از'), so 'کز اندیشه بد' = 'from (or because of) bad thought'. 'مکن' is the negative imperative 'do not'. 'یاد هیچ' ('remember nothing') places the noun 'یاد' and quantifier 'هیچ' together; the negative imperative intervenes ('مکن'), giving the sense 'do not call to mind anything because of bad thought.' Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s071-g152: 'دلت' is 'دل' + 2nd‑person possessive suffix 'ت' = 'your heart'. 'شاد کن' is an imperative construction meaning 'make (it) glad' (شاد = happy, کن = do/make). The following noun phrase 'کار مهمان بسیچ' means 'much work/deeds for the guest' (بسیچ as quantifier 'much/many'); the line commands both inward cheer ('make your heart glad') and active hospitality ('do many deeds for the guest').

sentences.s071-s33: You, humble attendant of Mehrab — tell me: where did Zal see that maiden of his?
sentences.s071-s34: Tell me about her face, her hair, her disposition and her wit, so that no anxiety may arise.
sentences.s071-s35: Of his stature, his meeting (appearance) and his upbringing — describe them so, as you saw, one by one.
sentences.s071-s36: Sindukht said to him, 'O hero — head of the champions and bulwark of men,'
sentences.s071-s9: He kissed the ground and cried “Bravo!” — praise to the king and to the hero of the land.
sentences.s071-s10: Gifts and worshippers and steeds and elephants were set in ranks, drawn out from the gate for two miles.
sentences.s071-s11: Bring each and every one before Sam; the champion was struck dumb when he saw that.
sentences.s071-s12: He sat down full of thought, like a drunk; he drew back his hand and stood abashed and humbled.
sentences.s071-s17: Take this and hand it into the treasury; give it in the name of Mah of Kabulistan.
sentences.s071-s18: Fair-faced Sindukht stood before Sam; she spoke eloquently and her heart was joyful.
sentences.s071-s19: When she saw those gifts accepted, she saw that good had come and evil had gone.
sentences.s071-s20: Three beauties were with her in one place; they were jasmine-figured and tall as cypresses.
sentences.s071-s29: All the living people of the city could become his worshippers, and could abase themselves as the dust beneath his feet.
sentences.s071-s30: From that fear — which brought forth wit and strength — he created the radiant Nahid and Hvar.
sentences.s071-s31: Such a deed should not be approved by you; do not close the way by shedding blood.
sentences.s071-s32: To him Sam the hero said, 'Speak with me; I will ask about that coat — do not seek an excuse.'
sentences.s071-s57: You, together with Kabul and whoever is bound to you, remain joyful at heart and sound in body.
sentences.s071-s58: I am likewise of the same mind in this, that when Zal seeks Rudabeh from the world, he seeks a match.
sentences.s071-s59: Although you are of a different substance, nonetheless you will take that crown and throne.
sentences.s071-s60: Thus is the world: there is no grave shame in it; the Creator of the world does not shrink from battle.
sentences.s071-s1: When his work had been completed he sat down; then, when he girded a ring around the man's middle, he bound it.
sentences.s071-s2: One put a Roman rag on his head; another, at once, darted beneath him like the wind.
sentences.s071-s3: A band of boars came to Sam's court; they gave neither a cry nor spoke a name.
sentences.s071-s4: He told the officers so that, suddenly, they should proclaim it to the world's proud champion.
sentences.s071-s13: Where — in what place — was there provision for so many? What sort of custom was it to send a woman?
sentences.s071-s14: If I accept this request from him, the king's host will be displeased with me.
sentences.s071-s15: And if I return her to Zal's presence, he will raise wings like the Simurgh.
sentences.s071-s16: He lifted his head and said that this was the request: servants and elephants adorned.
sentences.s071-s41: I will strive to bring all the hidden treasures of Kabulistan to Zabulistan.
sentences.s071-s42: Aside from this, too, whatever thing may befall, let the wise chief learn of it from me.
sentences.s071-s43: At that time Sam took his hand in his, welcomed him kindly, and bound a covenant.
sentences.s071-s44: When Sindukht heard his oath, she found it to be his true word and his pledge.
sentences.s071-s53: Kabul, do not burn the hearts of the innocent; how can the ill‑fated come into the light of day?
sentences.s071-s54: When the hero heard the words from him, he saw a woman of wise counsel and bright spirit.
sentences.s071-s55: Her face was like spring and her height like a cypress; her middle like the sunset, and when she moved she scattered (charms).
sentences.s071-s56: Thus he answered: 'My vow is firm, even if it should cost my life.'
sentences.s071-s49: We have repeatedly cried “Afarin” to you; that same acclamation we raised to the sovereign, the king of the earth.
sentences.s071-s50: Now I have come to learn what your wish is; from Kabul tell me, who is your enemy and who your friend?
sentences.s071-s51: If we are sinners and of bad character, we do not belong in this kingship.
sentences.s071-s52: I am now before you, needy; O slayer—if you kill (me), or if you bind (me), then bind (me).
sentences.s071-s21: Each took a cup in his hand; he commanded that they come and sit in their places.
sentences.s071-s22: They rushed forward before the spahbed; all mingled with one another.
sentences.s071-s23: When they had settled the affair with the hero, they left the stranger's house.
sentences.s071-s24: Thus Sindokht said to the hero: 'With your counsel the old will become young.'
sentences.s071-s5: that a Kabul envoy came to the presence of the spahbed, the hero of Zabul.
sentences.s071-s6: A message gathered from Mehrab was brought to the spahbed Jahangir Sam.
